primary_contact_information.description_of_concern:
I am requesting a neighborhood traffic speed study for West Lyman Avenue between North Pennsylvania Avenue and North Denning Drive in Winter Park, FL. My family and I have lived at 621 W. Lyman Ave for just over seven years, and during that time we have noticed a steady increase in both the volume and the speed of cut-through traffic using our street as an alternative to Fairbanks Avenue.

Specific concerns:
1. **Excessive speeding:** Although the posted speed limit is 25 mph, it is very common to see vehicles traveling closer to 35–40 mph, especially during the morning (7:15–9:00 a.m.) and evening (4:30–6:30 p.m.) rush hours. I routinely walk my two children (ages 7 and 10) to a nearby bus stop, and we have had several close calls where cars came around the slight curve near our house much faster than expected. On at least three occasions this school year alone, I have had to physically pull my younger child back from the edge of the roadway because a car was approaching at what appeared to be well over the speed limit.

2. **Pedestrian and bicyclist safety:** Our block is heavily used by pedestrians, joggers, dog walkers, and bicyclists, including families with strollers and older residents who walk for exercise. There are stretches with no continuous sidewalk on one side, so people often have to walk along the edge of the pavement. When drivers are speeding or not paying attention, this creates a very unsafe situation. My neighbor across the street, who is in her late 60s, has mentioned that she no longer feels comfortable walking alone in the early evenings because of how fast some cars come through.

3. **Cut-through and commuter traffic:** Many drivers seem to use West Lyman Avenue as a shortcut to avoid congestion on Fairbanks Avenue and Morse Boulevard. This is particularly noticeable when there are events at Rollins College or in downtown Winter Park—traffic backs up on the main roads, and our residential street suddenly sees a surge of vehicles trying to bypass those backups. These drivers are often unfamiliar with the neighborhood and tend to accelerate quickly between stop signs.

4. **Near-miss incidents and property damage:** While I am not aware of any recent serious injuries, there have been several near-miss situations and at least two incidents of property damage. Last spring, a car traveling too fast clipped the side mirror of a parked vehicle in front of 609 W. Lyman Ave and did not stop. A couple of years ago, a driver lost control in the rain and went up onto the curb, knocking over our neighbor’s trash and recycling bins and leaving tire marks across their front yard. These incidents were never formally reported because no one was hurt, but they are symptomatic of a larger speeding problem.

5. **Noise and quality of life:** The frequent rapid acceleration and hard braking, especially from trucks and delivery vehicles, has increased noise levels on our block. We often hear engines revving late at night, and my children’s bedrooms face the street, so they are sometimes woken up by loud vehicles speeding by after 10 p.m.

We are not asking for any specific solution at this stage, but we are requesting that the City of Winter Park conduct a formal traffic speed and volume study on this segment of West Lyman Avenue. We would like data on average speeds, 85th percentile speeds, and daily traffic volumes during both weekday peak hours and weekends. Based on those findings, we hope the City can recommend appropriate traffic calming measures, such as speed humps, additional signage, pavement markings, or other strategies that align with existing City standards.

Several of us on the block have discussed this informally and are in agreement that the situation has gradually become more dangerous, particularly for children walking to bus stops and families accessing nearby parks. We appreciate the City’s help in evaluating whether current conditions justify additional traffic calming or enforcement so that our street can remain a safe residential area rather than an unofficial cut-through route.
